item.title: PEOPLE | Life on the line
item.description: When the tide is right and the weather is fine, Sereima Cakaunitabua is usually stationed where she enjoys being the most – the old Vugalei Bridge in Lami. The spot has become a second home for the Tai Vugalei, Tailevu woman, who has spent decades casting her line into the water’s edge.
item.content:encoded:
When the tide is right and the weather is fine, Sereima Cakaunitabua is usually stationed where she enjoys being the most – the old Vugalei Bridge in Lami.

The spot has become a second home for the Tai Vugalei, Tailevu woman, who has spent decades casting her line into the water’s edge.

Most days, fishing begins after breakfast, with her long time friend, Kelera Waqadau.

And by midday, when the scotching sun hovers above their heads, they are ready to head home.

Their day’s catch ends up in the dinner pot shared among friends or stored in the fridge for rainy days.

“I love fishing so when the weather is fine, I can be here every day of the week,” Sereima said.

“It’s better than just lazing around and doing nothing at home.”

As a young girl, she learned to fish by following the village women to where they stood waist-deep in the sea, patiently casting and gathering enough to nourish their families.

Years later, that lesson continues to help feed her family. Now living in Suvavou, where she is married, with her daughter and two grandchildren, she sees fishing as more than a hobby.

“Fishing is not for the faint-hearted. It requires a lot of patience and puts food on the table.”

“Sometimes you’d spend many hours fishing but would go back home with nothing at all, which happens. Sometimes there’s a lot of fish. There are good and bad days.”

Using sandworms called seasea, her usual daily catch includes matu, kaikai, kabatia and damu, that forage near the mangrove patch close to the bridge.

The fish end up on the family’s dinner table while the smaller ones are cooked for Nana, the family cat.

“Fishing provides me with some form of physical exercise and food for the family.”

“Life is tough, so this is one way I take care of my family and reduce my food expenses,” she said.

As long as the weather holds and the tide is right, she keeps returning to the bridge that has become a part of her daily routine.

“As long as there’s fish in the sea, you’d spot me on the bridge – rain or shine!”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: PEOPLE | Farm girl chases ocean dream
item.description: Growing up on a small farm just 10 minutes outside Lautoka, Janet Reddy often found herself living a very different childhood from many of her friends. While most of them lived in town, she was one of the few who grew up in a semi-rural setting, surrounded by nature.
item.content:encoded:
Growing up on a small farm just 10 minutes outside Lautoka, Janet Reddy often found herself living a very different childhood from many of her friends.

While most of them lived in town, she was one of the few who grew up in a semi-rural setting, surrounded by nature.

Today, that early connection to the environment has taken her from Fiji to one of the world’s leading universities, with the Cornell University graduate preparing to begin a PhD in Marine Science and Conservation at Duke University in North Carolina.

Although her academic path now seems clear, Janet said it was not always the direction she envisioned for herself.

When she first left Fiji to study in the United States, she admitted she was uncertain about her future. It was an experience from her childhood, however, that would ultimately shape her life’s work.

Cyclone Winston, which devastated Fiji in 2016, became an epoch moment for her.

“I remember reading the news afterwards and seeing that big countries were still debating whether climate change was real,” she recalled.

“It just seemed ridiculous that climate change was a lived reality for so many of us, while countries that could do something about it were doing nothing.”

That experience ignited her passion for environmental conservation, particularly the protection of the ocean, an area she says has always fascinated her.

Her years at Cornell, together with research opportunities and international travel, have broadened her understanding of conservation beyond the classroom.

An exchange semester in South Africa, where she lived with a local family in a rural village, revealed striking similarities with her own upbringing in Fiji and reinforced the importance of place-based knowledge in protecting natural ecosystems.

Despite studying abroad, Janet highlighted that she has always felt a responsibility to return home and contribute to Fiji.

“I knew that not many people from a small island nation had access to the opportunities I was getting,” she said.

“I knew that I wanted to come back home eventually and give back in some capacity.”

Looking ahead, she hopes her doctoral research will help address gaps in Fiji’s marine science, particularly the study of humpback whales, which migrate through Fijian waters each year but remain largely unresearched.

Janet’s decision to journey far from the familiar comforts of her small family farm outside Lautoka to one of the world’s leading research institutions was never just about academic attainment.

Her pursuit is shaped by a genuine desire to gain the knowledge and experience needed to help protect the natural world which first inspired her and ultimately bring back much needed wisdom to the place she has always called home.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: PEOPLE | From Batiki to the world
item.description: Adi Salaseini Muavesi Lesianawai has seen the sea as more than just a source of livelihood, but a pathway that has helped shape her dreams. Raised in Yavu Village on Batiki Island during her primary school years, Adi Salaseini grew up in a community where travelling by boat was simply part of everyday life.
item.content:encoded:
Adi Salaseini Muavesi Lesianawai has seen the sea as more than just a source of livelihood, but a pathway that has helped shape her dreams.

Raised in Yavu Village on Batiki Island during her primary school years, Adi Salaseini grew up in a community where travelling by boat was simply part of everyday life.

It was this close relationship with the ocean that inspired a lifelong passion for the maritime sector and ultimately led her to pursue a career dedicated to protecting Fiji’s waters.

Today, she is an integral part of the team at the Maritime Safety Authority of Fiji (MSAF), where she contributes to promoting safe and secure maritime transport while helping safeguard the nation’s marine environment.

Her commitment to the field has now earned international recognition after graduating with a Master of Laws (LL.M.) in International Maritime Law from the prestigious International Maritime Law Institute (IMLI).

Adding to the milestone, she was selected to deliver the valedictory address on behalf of the IMLI Class of 2025-2026 and was awarded the institute’s Prize for Best Performance, recognising her academic excellence and leadership.

Although she proudly traces her paternal heritage to Vuna, Taveuni, Adi Salaseini’s maternal roots connect her to Yavu Village, where she attended Batiki District School and spent much of her childhood.

Family holidays and gatherings have ensured her connection with the village has remained strong throughout the years.

Her achievement has been warmly celebrated by the people of Yavu, who describe her success as an inspiration for young people across Batiki, Lomaiviti and Fiji.

Adi Salaseini’s commitment is also a reflection that determination, humility and faith can overcome the challenges often faced by children growing up in remote island communities.

While her work in international maritime law continues now to the next level, her outstanding achievement, which no doubt becomes an invaluable asset to Fiji is proof that “big ocean states” like Fiji are more than capable of producing impact leaders on the global stage, while at the same time remaining firmly anchored to the grassroots that helped shape their ambitions.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: PEOPLE | Coral Coast to Australia’s big stage
item.description: THE first stage Atelaite Ruci Tuiwai ever stood on was not in a concert hall or a packed arena. It was in a Methodist Church of Fiji conference, where a three-year-old girl sang alongside a choir led by her late father.
item.content:encoded:
THE first stage Atelaite Ruci Tuiwai ever stood on was not in a concert hall or a packed arena.

It was in a Methodist Church of Fiji conference, where a three-year-old girl sang alongside a choir led by her late father.

Years later, that same girl would find herself performing as a backing vocalist for Australian music star Guy Sebastian, singing with Stan Walker and Bella Taylor, and taking the stage at Sydney Recital Hall.

Yet despite the remarkable journey, the woman known as RUCI on stage says her greatest foundation was built long before the spotlight found her.

Originally from Lomaji in Kabara, Lau, with maternal links to Nadrala, Conua and Baravi in Nadroga, Ruci has called Australia home for the past 12 years. But she says Fiji has never left her heart.

“My identity is deeply rooted in my family, my faith and my vanua. Being iTaukei is more than just knowing where I’m come from; it reflects the values I carry through respect, humility, service and the responsibility of representing those who came before us,” she said.

Much of Ruci’s childhood was spent along Fiji’s Coral Coast, where her late father served as a vakatawa (lay pastor) under the Methodist Church of Fiji for 16 years.

She describes him as far more than a parent.

“My father wasn’t just my dad, he was my mentor, my first music teacher and my biggest supporter,” she said.

“Music was part of our everyday life, and through him I learned that singing was never just about performing; it was about serving God and serving people.”

Those early lessons became the cornerstone of a musical journey that continues to unfold.

Ruci’s debut gospel single, Me’u Muri Ga, was co-composed and recorded with Lesaa. She later featured with VLY on the remake of the classic Fijian song O Nadroga Okwe Kodaki o Amerika from Na Vucu ni Tauvu, before collaborating with the Domo ni Cakau group on Sevataki Dina Sara, a remake of the Black Rose classic.

Her career has since expanded beyond the Fijian music scene.

She has performed as a backing vocalist for Guy Sebastian during his tour, joined the backing choir for Stan Walker and Bella Taylor at the One Sydney event, and sang choir backing vocals for Voli K at the Sydney Recital Hall, where she was also entrusted with a solo performance.

“Each opportunity has taught me something different and continues to shape me as a musician and as a person,” she said.

Rather than viewing the opportunity as luck, RUCI believes it was the result of years of quiet preparation.

“Looking back, I can see that God was preparing me long before I received that opportunity,” she said.

“It reminded me that sometimes the doors we walk through are the result of seasons of preparation that we didn’t even realise were preparing us.”

Full story in The Sunday Times this weekend

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: PEOPLE I Brewing dreams beyond the aroma
item.description: The aroma of freshly brewed coffee fills the Suva air long before the morning rush sets in. Behind the espresso machine, Timoci Qoro moves with confidence, greeting customers with an easy smile before crafting another cup that has become part of someone else’s daily routine.
item.content:encoded:
The aroma of freshly brewed coffee fills the Suva air long before the morning rush sets in.

Behind the espresso machine, Timoci Qoro moves with confidence, greeting customers with an easy smile before crafting another cup that has become part of someone else’s daily routine.

Few would guess the 27-year-old barista is chasing a very different dream.

Originally from Namoli Village in Lautoka, with maternal links to Tubou in Lakeba, Lau, Qoro never trained as a barista.

Everything he knows about coffee has been learned on the job at The Republic of Cappuccino in Suva.

“I didn’t go to school to become a barista,” he said.

“I learned by watching, practising and asking questions. Every day, there’s something new to pick up.”

Coffee, he says, is more than just a drink. It is part of a culture.

“It gives people a reason to slow down. Friends meet over coffee, families catch up and strangers start conversations. It’s amazing how one cup can bring people together.”

While he enjoys creating that experience for customers, his heart has always belonged to the ebbing sea.

A part-time Fiji National University maritime school student, Qoro dreams of one day becoming a ship captain. It is a childhood ambition that never faded, despite having no family background in the industry.

“I’ve always wanted to explore the world. Being a captain means travelling becomes part of the job. That’s something I’ve held onto since I was young.”

The second youngest of seven siblings, he now works part-time to save enough money to return to his studies and complete his Class 3 Watchkeeper Certificate of Competency.

For now, every shift behind the coffee machine is another step towards that goal.

“You have to work hard and support yourself,” he said.

“Being independent means making good decisions today so you can reach where you want to be tomorrow.”

Until the day he stands on the bridge of a ship, Qoro will continue serving the best coffee money can buy at The ROC. with the same purpose that fuels his maritime dream.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: PEOPLE | Breaking new ground
item.description: A CAREER in hydrology was never part of Margaret Tabutoa’s original plan. Yet today, as the only woman serving in the hydrology division of the Fiji Meteorological Service (FMS), she has become a trailblazer in a profession where women remain few.
item.content:encoded:
A CAREER in hydrology was never part of Margaret Tabutoa’s original plan.

Yet today, as the only woman serving in the hydrology division of the Fiji Meteorological Service (FMS), she has become a trailblazer in a profession where women remain few.

Originally from Levuka, Ovalau, with family links to Vanuavatu in Lau, Margaret grew up in Suva and attended Suva Grammar School before pursuing a Trade Diploma in Telecommunication and Networking Engineering.

Engineering itself appealed to her because it was a field dominated by men.

“I wanted to study engineering because there were very few females in the engineering field,” she said, crediting her parents for encouraging her to pursue a challenging career path.

After spending three years searching for work, she joined the Fiji Meteorological Service in 2018 as a technical assistant officer in the Department of Hydrology. Looking back, she believes the opportunity came at exactly the right time.

“I’d like to thank God for this job because He was the main reason I came into this organisation,” she said.

What began as an unfamiliar profession soon became a passion.

Seven years with the Western Division helped shape her expertise before she was transferred to Suva last August and promoted to Technical Officer II Hydrology.

Today, she helps oversee streamflow measurements and water-level monitoring across the Central Division, supporting a network of 13 monitoring stations while planning for future expansion.

Although the technical work is rewarding, Margaret said working as the only woman in the division has brought its own challenges.

“When I first joined, there were only two females in hydrology. Now it’s just me,” she said.

Among her proudest moments has been working alongside colleagues to organise World Meteorological Day activities and raising public awareness about the importance of hydrology throughout Fiji.

She hopes her journey will encourage more young women to enter engineering and hydrology.

“Hydrology offers a great experience, and internationally it’s a highly demanding profession,” she said.

“We need both men and women working together. It’s teamwork.”

Margaret firmly believes that breaking barriers is less about making history and more about opening doors for the next generation of women to step confidently into fields once considered beyond their reach.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: PEOPLE | Woman behind the scenes: Muavesi serves her vanua with a deep sense of pride
item.description: While chiefs took their places on the main dais and traditional ceremonies unfolded before hundreds gathered for the Lau Provincial Council meeting in Suva, much of the event’s success rested on the quiet dedication of people working behind the scenes. Among them is Muavesi, of Yaroi Village, Matuku, one of many women whose months of preparation helped ensure the historic gathering ran seamlessly.
item.content:encoded:
While chiefs took their places on the main dais and traditional ceremonies unfolded before hundreds gathered for the Lau Provincial Council meeting in Suva, much of the event’s success rested on the quiet dedication of people working behind the scenes.

Among them is Muavesi, of Yaroi Village, Matuku, one of many women whose months of preparation helped ensure the historic gathering ran seamlessly.

“This year’s Bose ni Yasana ko Lau carries special significance for Matuku, making the occasion one of celebration and renewal for its people.”

For Muavesi, the event represents the culmination of months of planning, sacrifice and teamwork.

She said preparations had begun last year, with members of the Matuku community working collectively to fulfil their responsibilities to the vanua.

“We are very happy and pleased with how our preparations have unfolded,” she said.

Although the meeting was initially planned to be held on Matuku Island, organising the event in Suva presented an entirely different set of challenges.

Many Matuku people involved in the preparations live and work in the capital, requiring them to juggle full-time employment while volunteering countless hours to coordinate logistics, ceremonies and hospitality.

Despite the obstacles, Muavesi said the support from those back home never wavered.

Villagers contributed fish, root crops and traditional presentations, while families worked together to prepare the mats, masi and ceremonial decorations used to welcome the paramount chief of Lau, the Tui Nayau, Ratu Tevita Uliulakeba Mara and President and Tui Cakau Ratu Naiqama Lalabalavu.

She said the collective effort reflected the enduring spirit of “Matuku Dua” — the unity for which the district is known.

“There were challenges along the way, but we learnt valuable lessons from them. We tried our utmost best to ensure everything was complete and nothing was left out,” she said.

Hearing Ratu Naiqama commend Matuku’s organisation following Tuesday’s opening ceremony brought a deep sense of pride to everyone involved.

Muavesi reserved special praise for the women of the island, whose tireless work often goes unnoticed.

She thanked them for their commitment and for ensuring every aspect behind the scenes flowed smoothly.

As the provincial meeting concludes today, Muavesi said the people of Matuku remained committed to carrying out their traditional duties with excellence — honouring both their chiefs and a milestone that will be remembered for generations.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: PEOPLE I Tourism tides shift
item.description: Mahendra Singh has spent more than six decades watching the tides of tourism rise and fall in Suva. From his stall at the Suva Handicraft Centre, he has witnessed the capital transform from a bustling cruise destination into a city where, he believes, too few visitors now venture beyond pre-arranged tours. At nearly 78 years old, the president of the Suva Handicraft Market Vendors and Manufacturer
item.content:encoded:
Mahendra Singh has spent more than six decades watching the tides of tourism rise and fall in Suva. From his stall at the Suva Handicraft Centre, he has witnessed the capital transform from a bustling cruise destination into a city where, he believes, too few visitors now venture beyond pre-arranged tours.

At nearly 78 years old, the president of the Suva Handicraft Market Vendors and Manufacturers Association remains a passionate advocate for Fiji’s traditional crafts and the people whose livelihoods depend on them.

Born in 1948 and raised on Lakeba St in Suva, Mr Singh attended Suva Primary School before studying accountancy at the then Fiji Institute of Technology. But finding work was difficult, prompting him to take a different path.

As a 13-year-old accompanying his mother to the old Municipal Market, he noticed the steady stream of tourists arriving from cruise ships and recognised an opportunity. He found work selling handicrafts, beginning a career that would define his life.

For 16 years, he worked under another trader before securing his own stall when vendors relocated to the newly established Suva Handicraft Centre in 1979.

The move, he recalls, came only after lengthy negotiations with the Suva City Council. Vendors insisted that street trading of handicrafts cease so everyone could operate under one roof.

Mr Singh remembers those early years as the centre’s golden era.

Cruise liners, particularly those operated by P&O, arrived regularly, bringing thousands of visitors into the city. Business flourished, allowing artisans to earn an income while showcasing authentic Fijian craftsmanship.

Over the years, he developed his own range of handcrafted wooden artefacts, many of which still fill his stall today. Large tanoa, carved wooden pieces and other traditional items remain on display, each reflecting skills passed down through generations.

“We are not only selling Fijian handicrafts. While we are selling our products here, we are promoting the Fijian culture at the same time.,” he said.

He believes every purchase represents more than a sale. Handmade artefacts, crafted over weeks or even months, preserve traditions that survive in only a handful of communities, particularly Kabara and Fulaga in the Lau Group.

Although age has slowed his own carving, Mr Singh continues to champion the industry. He worries that cruise passengers increasingly bypass Suva’s city centre and that local artisans are missing opportunities to benefit from tourism.

He maintains that the Suva Handicraft Centre is more than a marketplace. It is a living showcase of Fiji’s cultural identity, where every handmade artefact tells a story of craftsmanship, heritage and resilience that deserves to endure for generations to come.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
